title: Recovery/Restart/Retry Red-Team Attack — Phase 3-2 Changes
attacks_attempted: Partial IPC write — main handler crashes mid-invoke, stale ipcMain registration
Duplicate send — rapid pluginSend/pluginInvoke, queue vs drop
getUpdaterEnabled() runtime failure — app undefined at call time
Stale meta.ts import — dynamic require or path reference
Concurrent plugin load — IPC channel collision
Manual unsub followed by destroy — double-call of ipcRenderer.removeListener
pluginOff no-op — documented no-op violates PluginTransport contract
Module-level handler registry — no lifecycle cleanup for dead plugins
Broadcast push — sendPluginPush sends to ALL BrowserWindows, no per-window isolation
attack_surface: packages/desktop/src/main/plugin-transport-ipc.ts — IPC handler registration, routing, push broadcast
packages/desktop/src/preload/index.ts — pluginSend, pluginOn, pluginOff, pluginInvoke bridge
packages/desktop/src/main/constants.ts — getUpdaterEnabled() as function instead of module-level const

findings.title: Manual unsubscribe followed by destroy() calls ipcRenderer.removeListener twice
findings.attack_vector: Double destroy — subscription tracking bug in createElectronPluginTransport()
findings.proof: plugin-transport.ts:on() stores `unsub` from `api.pluginOn()` in `activeSubscriptions`. The returned `cleanup` closure calls `activeSubscriptions.delete(cleanup)` — but `cleanup` was never added to the Set; `unsub` was. A manually-unsubscribed handler's `unsub` remains in `activeSubscriptions`. When `destroy()` iterates and calls all, it calls `unsub()` a second time. File: packages/desktop/src/renderer/plugin-transport.ts lines 66-73.
findings.repair_steps: Change `activeSubscriptions.add(unsub)` to `activeSubscriptions.add(cleanup)` so the Set contains the closure that both calls unsub() AND removes itself. Then `activeSubscriptions.delete(cleanup)` in the cleanup function matches the right reference. Alternatively, wrap both: `activeSubscriptions.add(() => { unsub(); activeSubscriptions.delete(cleanup) })`.

findings.title: Main-process handler registry has no lifecycle cleanup for dead plugin channels
findings.attack_vector: Stale state — module-level Maps persist handlers after plugin unload
findings.proof: plugin-transport-ipc.ts lines 40-43: `messageHandlers` and `invokeHandlers` are module-level Maps with no lifecycle hook. If a plugin's lifecycle ends without calling the returned unsub functions, its handlers remain registered. A new plugin that reuses the same channel triggers both old and new handlers. Unlike the renderer-side transport (which has destroy()), the main-process registry has no destroy() equivalent. File: packages/desktop/src/main/plugin-transport-ipc.ts lines 40-43, 49-85.
findings.repair_steps: Add a `clearPluginHandlers(pluginName: string)` function that unregisters all handlers whose channel starts with `pluginName:`. Call this from the plugin lifecycle teardown path. Alternatively, wrap handler sets with a WeakRef-based or session-scoped lifecycle so that when the plugin's BrowserWindow is destroyed, its handlers auto-deregister.

findings.title: sendPluginPush broadcasts to all BrowserWindows with no recipient isolation
findings.attack_vector: Concurrent plugin load — cross-window message bleed
findings.proof: plugin-transport-ipc.ts lines 88-97: `sendPluginPush()` iterates ALL windows and broadcasts. While channel prefixing (pluginName:) prevents accidental cross-plugin leakage within a single window, there is no window-level isolation. If window A has Plugin X and window B has a different Plugin Y, a push intended for X goes to all windows. Any window's PLUGIN_PUSH ipcRenderer listener fires, and the channel match decides if the handler processes it. This adds unnecessary ipcRenderer message processing in unintended windows. File: packages/desktop/src/main/plugin-transport-ipc.ts lines 88-97, packages/desktop/src/preload/index.ts lines 113-119.
findings.repair_steps: Accept a target window ID(s) parameter in sendPluginPush() instead of broadcasting to all windows. Alternatively, maintain a Map<number, BrowserWindow> of plugin-registered windows and only push to those that have registered a handler on that channel. Document that the current pattern is broadcast-only.

findings.title: pluginOff is a documented no-op — violates PluginTransport interface contract
findings.attack_vector: Duplicate send — memory leak if caller relies on off()
findings.proof: preload/index.ts lines 120-122: `pluginOff` is `(_channel, _handler) => { /* no-op */ }`. The comment says 'IPC push removal is handled by the returned unsub function from pluginOn'. The PluginTransport interface (plugin/src/desktop.ts line 31) declares `off()` with no indication it could be a no-op. A caller not holding the unsub reference from `on()` cannot unsubscribe. If a plugin calls `transport.off(channel, handler)` expecting cleanup, the handler persists forever. File: packages/desktop/src/preload/index.ts lines 120-122.
findings.repair_steps: Implement pluginOff properly by tracking (channel → Set<handler>) mappings in the preload bridge and removing the correct listener from ipcRenderer. Alternatively, throw an error in pluginOff to signal the pattern mismatch, or remove it from the Electron preload bridge entirely and update the PluginTransport docs.

findings.title: Rapid concurrent pluginInvoke calls can interleave on shared handler state
findings.attack_vector: Duplicate send — no reentrancy guard on invoke handlers
findings.proof: plugin-transport-ipc.ts: `ipcMain.handle(IPC.handle.PLUGIN_INVOKE, ...)` allows concurrent invocations from the same or different renderers. `handlePluginInvoke()` reads the first handler from `invokeHandlers` and awaits its return. If the handler internally accesses shared mutable state (e.g., an in-memory counter, a cache), rapid invocations interleave. The transport layer provides no reentrancy documentation or guard. File: packages/desktop/src/main/plugin-transport-ipc.ts lines 29-34, 114-126.
findings.repair_steps: Document that plugin invoke handlers must be reentrant-safe. Optionally add a per-channel mutex or queue (p-limit or simple semaphore) that serializes invocations on the same channel. This prevents accidental shared-state corruption at the cost of throughput.

findings.title: Partial IPC handler crash leaves ipcMain registration clean but intermediate state lost
findings.proof: `ipcMain.on(IPC.send.PLUGIN_SEND, ...)` catches per-handler errors in `handlePluginMessage` (try/catch swallows). `ipcMain.handle(IPC.handle.PLUGIN_INVOKE, ...)` wraps in `withIpcResult()` which catches all errors and returns a structured error envelope. If the main process OOMs or segfaults mid-invoke, the ipcMain registration persists (it's module-level and survives reload). The renderer-side invoke() in plugin-transport.ts has its own try/catch for `api.pluginInvoke`. No realistic state corruption scenario exists beyond what already kills the process. File: packages/desktop/src/main/plugin-transport-ipc.ts lines 22-34, 101-112, 114-126; packages/desktop/src/renderer/plugin-transport.ts lines 40-50.
findings.repair_steps: Add a per-channel timeout (e.g., 30s) to handlePluginInvoke so that a never-resolving handler doesn't leak a pending promise in ipcMain.handle. Document the crash resilience in the transport layer — per-handler isolation is already adequate.

findings.title: getUpdaterEnabled() depends on module-level `import { app } from 'electron'` preventing browser usage
findings.attack_vector: getUpdaterEnabled() runtime failure in non-Electron context
findings.proof: constants.ts line 1 imports `{ app }` from electron at module level. Even though `getUpdaterEnabled()` is now a function (NOT evaluated at module load time), the `app` import itself fails when the module is loaded in a browser/Node.js test context. This is consistent with every other file in packages/desktop/src/main/ (updater.ts, menu.ts, etc. all import from electron directly). The refactor from `const UPDATER_ENABLED` to `function getUpdaterEnabled()` correctly deferred app.isPackaged evaluation from module load to call time, but cannot eliminate the electron import dependency. File: packages/desktop/src/main/constants.ts lines 1,12-14.
findings.repair_steps: Extract the `app.isPackaged` check into a parameter or allow injection of a PlatformPaths-like interface. For now, document that getUpdaterEnabled() is main-process-only. A future PlatformPaths consolidation (DC-001 follow-up) should push isPackaged through the abstraction so constants.ts doesn't import electron directly.

findings.title: meta.ts deletion is clean — no stale static or dynamic imports remain
findings.proof: Grep for `from './meta'`, `from '../meta'`, `require('.*meta')`, and `import('.*meta')` in packages/desktop/ returns zero results. Git log for `--diff-filter=D` on meta.ts and meta.test* returns no commits (was deleted in a previous session). File: packages/desktop/src/main/meta.ts — confirmed absent.
findings.repair_steps: None required. Verify with `rg '\./meta' packages/desktop/src/` in CI if meta.ts is permanently removed.

findings.title: import.meta.env.OPENCODE_CHANNEL in constants.ts has no build-time validation
findings.attack_vector: Retry — env var not set at compile time
findings.proof: constants.ts line 5: `const raw = import.meta.env.OPENCODE_CHANNEL`. This is a Vite/ESBuild compile-time substitution. If the build pipeline omits the substitution, `raw` is `undefined`, and CHANNEL defaults to `'dev'`. In a release build, this silently disables auto-update (`getUpdaterEnabled()` → `app.isPackaged && 'dev' !== 'dev'` → always `false`). Not introduced by this phase, but related to the refactored `getUpdaterEnabled()`. File: packages/desktop/src/main/constants.ts lines 5-7.
findings.repair_steps: Add a build-time assertion or fallback detection: if `import.meta.env.OPENCODE_CHANNEL` is undefined at production build time, fail the build. Alternatively, derive the channel from `app.isPackaged` and app identity at runtime instead of compile time.

repaired_seams.seam: getUpdaterEnabled converted from module-level const to function
repaired_seams.outcome: VERIFIED — app.isPackaged now evaluated at call time, eliminating the circular-reference timing hazard identified in the DC-001 review. Consumers (index.ts, updater.ts, menu.ts) all call the function instead of importing the const.
VERIFIED — zero stale static imports, zero dynamic imports, zero require() references remain.
VERIFIED — destroy() iterates and clears activeSubscriptions. NoopTransport destroy() is safe no-op. Double-destroy is safe (empty Set iteration is a no-op).
VERIFIED — checks `window.api?.pluginSend` before creating Electron transport. No-op fallback prevents uncaught errors in browser context.
VERIFIED — prevents cross-plugin collision on the wire. Tested in plugin-transport-bridge.test.ts and plugin-transport.test.ts.

surviving_weaknesses: No lifecycle cleanup in main-process handler registry (Finding 2)
Manual unsub tracking bug in activeSubscriptions (Finding 1)
pluginOff is a no-op violating interface contract (Finding 4)
sendPluginPush broadcasts to all windows without isolation (Finding 3)
No reentrancy documentation or guard for concurrent invoke handlers (Finding 5)

recommendations.action: Fix activeSubscriptions tracking bug — store `cleanup` closure instead of raw `unsub`
Add `clearPluginHandlers(pluginName)` to main-process handler registry and wire it to plugin lifecycle teardown
Implement proper `pluginOff` or document that it is intentionally unsupported and will throw
Add per-channel mutex or documentation for handler reentrancy in invoke path
Add target window isolation to sendPluginPush or document broadcast semantics

architectural_proposals.title: Session-scoped IPC handler registry with automatic cleanup
architectural_proposals.detail: Replace module-level `messageHandlers`/`invokeHandlers` Maps with a registry bound to a BrowserWindow or plugin session lifecycle. Use a Map<number, Map<string, Set<Handler>>> keyed by webContents.id so that when a window is destroyed, its entire handler tree auto-deregisters. This eliminates stale-handler leaks without requiring manual unsub tracking.

architectural_proposals.title: Command-query separation with idempotency keys in plugin transport
architectural_proposals.detail: Add an optional `idempotencyKey` field to PLUGIN_SEND/PLUGIN_INVOKE messages. The main-process registry deduplicates by key within a configurable TTL window. This makes duplicate sends safe by construction — the same message sent twice (retry after timeout, restart replay) is a no-op on the second arrival. The key should be a (pluginName, channel, client-supplied-uuid) tuple.

architectural_proposals.title: Crash-recovery friendly PluginTransport with transactional delivery
architectural_proposals.detail: Wrap plugin message delivery in an append-only log (JSONL per plugin) before dispatching to handlers. On main process restart, replay undelivered messages from the log. Combined with idempotency keys, this turns the plugin transport from at-most-once (current) to at-least-once with dedup — making restart and recovery transparent to plugin handlers. The log can be pruned on TTL or after confirmed delivery.
